questions.question: Under Irish law, an express trust of personal property (personalty):

options.A: Must be created in writing and signed by the settlor
options.B: Can be created orally without any formalities, provided the three certainties are satisfied
options.C: Requires a deed executed before two independent witnesses
options.D: Must be registered with the Revenue Commissioners to be valid

questions.explanation: Under Irish law, an express trust of personalty (personal property) can be created orally without any formalities, provided the three certainties are satisfied. Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695 requires writing only for trusts of land, not for trusts of personal property.

questions.question: Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695 requires that a declaration of trust over land must be:

options.A: Made in writing and executed as a deed
options.B: Manifested and proved by some writing signed by the party who is by law enabled to declare such trust
options.C: Evidenced by at least two witnesses who were present at the declaration
options.D: Registered in the Land Registry before taking effect

questions.explanation: Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695 requires that a declaration of trust over land must be 'manifested and proved by some writing signed by the party who is by law enabled to declare such trust'. Critically, the trust does not have to be created in writing — it must be manifested and proved by writing. An oral trust of land is not void but merely unenforceable without written evidence.

questions.question: What is the legal effect of an oral declaration of trust over land that is not evidenced in writing under Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695?

options.A: The trust is void and of no legal effect
options.B: The trust is unenforceable unless there is sufficient written evidence, but is not void
options.C: The trust is valid but can only be enforced against the original trustee, not successors
options.D: The trust automatically converts into a resulting trust in favour of the settlor

questions.explanation: An oral declaration of trust over land that is not evidenced in writing under Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds is not void — it is merely unenforceable. If there is subsequently sufficient written evidence of the trust, it can be enforced. This distinction between void and unenforceable is critical for FE-1 candidates.

questions.question: Which trusts are expressly excluded from the writing requirement in Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds?

options.A: Charitable trusts and trusts for public purposes
options.B: Testamentary trusts created by will
options.C: Resulting, implied, and constructive trusts
options.D: Trusts created by statute or statutory instrument

questions.explanation: Resulting, implied, and constructive trusts are expressly excluded from the writing requirement in Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds. These trusts arise by operation of law, and the legislature recognised that requiring writing for informally arising trusts would be unjust — it would allow the Statute to be used as an instrument of fraud.

questions.question: The case of Rochefoucauld v Boustead [1897] is authority for the proposition that:

options.A: An oral trust of land is always valid even without written evidence
options.B: Equity will not permit the Statute of Frauds to be used as an instrument of fraud, enforcing an oral trust through a constructive trust
options.C: A constructive trust can only arise where there is actual fraud by the trustee
options.D: The three certainties do not apply to constructive trusts

questions.explanation: Rochefoucauld v Boustead [1897] is authority for the principle that equity will not permit the Statute of Frauds to be used as an instrument of fraud. Where property is conveyed on an oral undertaking to hold it on trust, and the transferee then relies on the absence of writing to deny the trust, equity will impose a constructive trust to prevent the fraud — even in the absence of the required writing.

questions.question: What is the legal consequence where a settlor declares themselves trustee of their own property for the benefit of another?

options.A: The trust is not constituted until the settlor executes a deed of transfer to a third party trustee
options.B: The trust is completely constituted the moment the declaration is made because no change of legal title is needed
options.C: The declaration must be registered with the Charities Regulator to take effect
options.D: A self-declaration of trust requires the consent of the beneficiary before it is effective

questions.explanation: A self-declaration of trust occurs when the owner of property declares themselves a trustee for another. The trust is completely constituted the moment the declaration is made because the settlor already holds the legal title — there is no transfer required. This was confirmed in Paul v Paul [1882]. No change of legal title is needed since the settlor becomes the trustee.

questions.question: A transfer on trust, as distinct from a self-declaration of trust, involves:

options.A: The settlor declaring themselves trustee and immediately vesting legal title in the beneficiary
options.B: The settlor transferring legal title to a separate trustee who holds the property on trust for the beneficiaries
options.C: A statutory transfer of property by a court order in family law proceedings
options.D: The beneficiary acquiring legal title directly from the settlor by virtue of the trust

questions.explanation: A transfer on trust involves the settlor transferring legal title to a separate trustee who holds the property on trust for the beneficiaries. The trust is not completely constituted until the legal title has actually been transferred to the trustee. If the transfer is never completed, the trust may fail — which engages the doctrine of constitution covered in Module 3.

questions.question: The beneficiary principle, established in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804], provides that:

options.A: Every trust must have identifiable beneficiaries who can enforce it
options.B: Every beneficiary must have a fixed and ascertainable share in the trust property
options.C: Beneficiaries must be of full legal capacity before they can enforce a trust
options.D: The number of beneficiaries must not exceed the number of trustees

questions.explanation: The beneficiary principle, established in Morice v Bishop of Durham [1804], provides that every private trust must have identifiable beneficiaries who can enforce it. Grant MR stated that every trust must have a definite object and there must be someone in whose favour the court can decree performance. This is also called the rule against trusts for purposes.

questions.question: Which of the following is an anomalous exception to the beneficiary principle recognised by the courts?

options.A: A trust for the establishment of a new public library
options.B: A trust for the maintenance of specific animals belonging to the testator
options.C: A trust for the benefit of future generations of the settlor's family
options.D: A trust for the promotion of political causes supported by the testator

questions.explanation: A trust for the maintenance of specific animals belonging to the testator is one of the recognised anomalous exceptions to the beneficiary principle. Other exceptions include trusts for the erection and maintenance of graves and monuments, and trusts for the saying of private masses. These are tolerated as historical anomalies but are construed narrowly and must not offend the rule against perpetuities.

questions.question: Why does the beneficiary principle require that there be someone to enforce the trust?

options.A: Because trustees are not legally obliged to act without a beneficiary's instructions
options.B: Because without a beneficiary, no one has standing to compel the trustee to carry out their obligations
options.C: Because equity only operates in personam and requires an identified person against whom it acts
options.D: Because the Trustee Act 1893 requires identification of all beneficiaries by name

questions.explanation: The practical reason for the beneficiary principle is that without a beneficiary, no one has standing to compel the trustee to carry out their obligations. The Attorney General enforces charitable trusts in the public interest, but for private purpose trusts, there is no one with standing to hold the trustees to account — hence the trust is void.

questions.question: An express trust is described as being created 'inter vivos'. This means:

options.A: The trust takes effect on the death of the settlor
options.B: The trust is created during the settlor's lifetime
options.C: The trust must have a fixed duration not exceeding the settlor's lifetime
options.D: The trust was created between co-living spouses

questions.explanation: An express trust created 'inter vivos' is one created during the settlor's lifetime, as opposed to a testamentary trust which takes effect on the testator's death. Inter vivos trusts are created by deed or declaration during the settlor's lifetime; testamentary trusts are created by will and take effect only on death.

questions.question: Paul v Paul [1882] confirmed which principle relating to express trusts?

options.A: That a transfer on trust requires registration to be effective
options.B: That a self-declaration of trust is effective immediately upon declaration without any transfer of title
options.C: That precatory words in a will cannot create a trust in personalty
options.D: That the three certainties must be satisfied before any trust can be enforced

questions.explanation: Paul v Paul [1882] confirmed that a self-declaration of trust is effective immediately upon declaration without any need to transfer legal title. Since the settlor already owns the property and has declared themselves trustee, the trust is constituted at that moment. There is no need for a separate transfer, making the trust complete from the moment of declaration.

questions.question: A settlor orally declares a trust over agricultural land in County Tipperary for the benefit of their grandchildren. What is the legal status of this trust under Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695?

options.A: Void — no oral trust of land is valid in Irish law
options.B: Valid and fully enforceable as a constructive trust
options.C: Unenforceable unless there is sufficient written evidence, but not void
options.D: Valid for personal property trusts only — it must be converted to a trust of personalty

questions.explanation: Under Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695, an oral declaration of trust over land is not void but is merely unenforceable unless manifested and proved by some writing signed by the party making the declaration. Without written evidence, the trust cannot be enforced, but if written evidence later emerges, it may be enforced.

questions.question: An anomalous non-charitable purpose trust is sometimes described as a 'trust of imperfect obligation'. This means:

options.A: The trust was imperfectly constituted and can be ratified by the beneficiaries
options.B: The trust is valid but unenforceable because there is no beneficiary with standing to compel the trustee
options.C: The trust is valid and enforceable but only for a period not exceeding 21 years
options.D: The trust's validity depends on whether the trustee has accepted the obligation in writing

questions.explanation: A trust of imperfect obligation is valid but unenforceable because there is no beneficiary with standing to compel the trustee. Such trusts — covering graves, monuments, and specific animals — are upheld as anomalies but rely on the trustee's willingness to perform. No court can be asked to enforce them for want of a beneficiary.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes an express trust?

options.A: A trust that arises automatically by operation of law without the intention of the parties
options.B: A trust that is deliberately and intentionally created by a settlor
options.C: A trust that arises where the settlor fails to exhaust the beneficial interest
options.D: A trust imposed by the court to prevent unconscionable conduct

questions.explanation: An express trust is one that is deliberately and intentionally created by a settlor — as opposed to trusts that arise by operation of law, such as resulting trusts (arising automatically from circumstances) or constructive trusts (imposed by courts to prevent unconscionability). Express trusts can be inter vivos or testamentary.

questions.question: The reason that Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds does not apply to resulting, implied, and constructive trusts is that:

options.A: These trusts are always created over personalty and not over land
options.B: Requiring writing for informally arising trusts would be unjust and would allow the Statute to be used as an instrument of fraud
options.C: These trusts are exclusively governed by the Trustee Act 1893 which overrides the Statute of Frauds
options.D: These trusts invariably arise in the context of commercial transactions where writing is impractical

questions.explanation: Resulting, implied, and constructive trusts are excluded from the writing requirement because they arise by operation of law in informal contexts. Requiring writing for such trusts would be unjust — it would allow the Statute to be used as an instrument of fraud, enabling a person to take advantage of a trust arrangement and then deny it existed for want of writing.

questions.question: An FE-1 exam question describes a person who orally told their sibling 'I am holding my Dublin apartment on trust for you'. Which statement is CORRECT?

options.A: The declaration is valid as an express trust because the three certainties are likely satisfied
options.B: The declaration requires writing to be enforceable as it concerns land under Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds
options.C: The declaration is immediately effective as a self-declaration of trust over personalty
options.D: The declaration is void because trusts of residential property can only be created by deed

questions.explanation: The declaration concerns land (an apartment) and therefore falls within Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds Ireland 1695. It requires writing to be enforceable — though it is not void without writing, merely unenforceable. Without written evidence signed by the declarant, the trust of the apartment cannot be enforced.

questions.question: In an exam question combining certainty issues, what is the CORRECT sequence for analysis?

options.A: First assess formalities, then subject matter, then intention, then objects
options.B: Work through all three certainties — intention, subject matter, objects — systematically before concluding
options.C: Begin with objects as this is the most commonly deficient certainty
options.D: Address only the most obviously deficient certainty to avoid overcomplicating the answer

questions.explanation: In an FE-1 exam question combining certainty issues, the correct approach is to work through all three certainties — intention, subject matter, and objects — systematically before reaching a conclusion. Each certainty must be satisfied independently. A trust may fail for more than one reason, and a thorough answer demonstrates awareness of all potential deficiencies.

questions.question: Which of the following trusts is an express trust created by will (testamentary)?

options.A: A father declares himself trustee of shares for his child during his lifetime
options.B: A testator leaves property to named trustees on trust for named beneficiaries under a will
options.C: A court imposes a constructive trust on a solicitor who misused client funds
options.D: A resulting trust arises when an express trust fails for uncertainty of objects

questions.explanation: A testamentary express trust is created by will and takes effect on the testator's death. Option B describes a testator leaving property to named trustees on trust for named beneficiaries under a will — this is the classic testamentary express trust. Options A, C, and D describe other types of trusts (inter vivos express, constructive, and resulting respectively).

questions.question: The rule against trusts for purposes is sometimes called 'the beneficiary principle'. Its practical effect is that a pure purpose trust — with no ascertainable beneficiaries — is:

options.A: Valid if the purpose is beneficial to the community
options.B: Void unless it falls within the recognised exceptions or qualifies as a charitable trust
options.C: Valid if the trustee agrees in writing to carry out the purpose
options.D: Valid for any purpose provided it does not offend public policy

questions.explanation: Under the beneficiary principle, a pure purpose trust with no ascertainable beneficiaries is void unless it falls within the recognised anomalous exceptions (graves, monuments, specific animals, private masses) or qualifies as a charitable trust (enforced by the Attorney General). There is no general validity for purpose trusts — the default position is invalidity.

questions.question: A settlor transfers legal title of shares to a trustee but the trustee refuses to act. What is the legal position of the trust?

options.A: The trust fails because the trustee's acceptance is required before the trust is constituted
options.B: The trust is constituted because legal title has passed to the trustee — equity will not allow a trust to fail for want of a trustee
options.C: Legal title reverts to the settlor automatically if the trustee refuses to act
options.D: The beneficiaries automatically become trustees in the trustee's place

questions.explanation: Once legal title has been transferred to the trustee, the trust is constituted — equity will not allow a trust to fail for want of a trustee. The maxim is that equity will not allow a trust to fail for want of a trustee. If the named trustee refuses to act or dies, the court may appoint a substitute trustee rather than allow the trust to fail.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid basis for equity enforcing an oral trust of land notwithstanding the absence of writing under the Statute of Frauds?

options.A: The imposition of a constructive trust to prevent the Statute being used as an instrument of fraud
options.B: Where the trust is a resulting trust arising by operation of law
options.C: Where the oral trust is supported by consideration paid by the beneficiary
options.D: Where subsequent acts of part performance render it unconscionable to deny the trust

questions.explanation: The presence of consideration does not independently exempt an oral trust of land from the Statute of Frauds writing requirement. The recognised bases for equity enforcing such trusts without writing are: (1) constructive trust to prevent fraud on the Statute (Rochefoucauld v Boustead), (2) resulting or constructive trusts arising by operation of law (expressly excluded by Section 4), and (3) part performance. Consideration alone does not create a trust nor override the formality requirement.
